coredata.dc:title: Phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dot-proteinase K nanohybrids for enhanced photodynamic therapy against bacterial biofilm infections
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Haoyi
Sun, Shan
Wang, Henggang
Cheng, Ke
Zhou, Yonghua
Wang, Xinxin
Gao, Shang
Mo, Jinhong
Li, Si
Lin, Hengwei
Wang, Peng
coredata.dc:description:
Nanohybrids combining phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dots (PCDs) and proteinase K have been engineered for addressing the formidable challenges of antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) against bacterial biofilm infections, overcoming biofilm barrier obstruction, the limited diffusion of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and the inadequate ROS generation of traditional photosensitizers. PCDs are formulated for superior water solubility and robust singlet oxygen (1O2) production, mitigating issues related to dispersion and aggregation-induced quenching typical of conventional photosensitizers. The conjugation of phenylboronic acid to CDs not only enhanced 1O2 generation through increased electron-hole separation but also imparted strong bacterial binding capabilities to the PCDs, enabling broad-spectrum sterilization by maximizing the ROS-mediated bacterial destruction. Proteinase K, serving as a structural “glue”, actively breaks down biofilms and facilitates the deep penetration of functional PCDs, aiding effective treatment of biofilm infections. In vivo studies confirm that PCDs-proteinase K nanohybrids dramatically accelerate healing in biofilm-infected wounds by synergizing enhanced photosensitization, potent bacterial adherence, and efficient biofilm elimination and penetration. This approach highlights a straightforward strategy to significantly advance aPDT, promoting the clinical adoption of non-antibiotic methods for combating bacterial biofilm infections.
Statement of significance 1) Phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dots (PCDs) were designed for enhanced water solubility and efficient singlet oxygen generation through surface modulation, also suggesting that surface modification can improve the inherent photosensitizing activity of CDs by promoting electron-hole separation; 2) The conjugation of phenylboronic acid endowed PCDs with strong bacterial binding capabilities, enabling highly efficient and broad-spectrum sterilization by maximizing reactive oxygen species-mediated bacterial destruction; 3) Incorporation of proteinase K (PK) leveraged its specific extracellular polymeric substance degrading capability, along with the stimuli-responsive release of PCDs from the PCDs-PK nanohybrids, facilitating biofilm breakdown and enabling deeper penetration of PCDs, thereby improving the treatment of biofilm infections
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dots
Proteinase K
Nanohybrids
Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy
Bacterial biofilm infections

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PCDs-PK nanohybrids
Enhanced photosensitization performance of PCDs and underlying mechanism
Photodynamic antibacterial activity of PCDs and the antibacterial mechanism
Biofilm elimination and penetration abilities of PCDs-PK nanohybrids
In vivo biofilm infection treatment performance of PCDs-PK nanohybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis process of PCDs-PK nanohybrids for enhanced photodynamic therapy against bacterial biofilm infections.

TEM images of a) PCDs, b) PCDs-PK nanohybrids, and c) biofilm-treated PCDs-PK nanohybrids (inset: particle size distribution histograms and HR-TEM images). d) FT-IR spectra, and e) Zeta potentials of CDs, PCDs and PCDs-PK nanohybrids. f) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, PCDs, PCDs-PK nanohybrids, PBA and PK. g) FL emission spectra of CDs, PCDs and PCDs-PK nanohybrids. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group).

a) Molecular changes in ABDA due to the generated 1O2. b) Absorbance decays of ABDA at 380 nm in the presence of CDs and PCDs under 660 nm laser irradiation (100 mW cm−2). c) Transient photocurrent response spectra, and d) EIS spectra of CDs and PCDs. e) Bandgap energies derived from Tauc plots for CDs, and f) PCDs. g) CV curves of CDs, and h) PCDs. i) Energy-band diagrams of CDs and PCDs.

a) Luria–Bertani (LB) agar plate photos of S. aureus, E. coli and P. aeruginosa after different treatments, and b-d) the corresponding statistical histograms of the antibacterial efficiency. e) Syto 9/ PI co-staining images of representative S. aureus and E. coli after different treatments. f) SEM images of S. aureus and E. coli after different treatments, highlighting typical bacterial membrane ruptures and morphological deformations (red arrows). g) Diagram depicting the interactions between PCDs and the teichoic acid/LPS of bacteria through borate ester covalent bonding. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001).

a) CLSM images of S. aureus and E. coli treated with CDs and PCDs, respectively, and b) corresponding quantitative analysis of FL intensity. c) Flow cytometry analysis of S. aureus and E. coli treated with CDs and PCDs, respectively. d) CLSM images of S. aureus and E. coli treated with saline, CDs, PCDs, saline+Laser, CDs+Laser and PCDs+Laser (660 nm Laser, 300 mW cm−2, 10 min), followed by staining with DCFH-DA, and d, f) corresponding quantitative analysis of FL intensity. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001).

a) Photos of crystal violet stained biofilms from different treatment groups, and b) corresponding quantitative analysis of biofilm absorbance. c) Syto 9/PI co-staining images of S. aureus biofilms after various treatments and visualized using the 3D function of CLSM. d) PI staining channel images of S. aureus biofilms at various depths following different treatments, and e) corresponding quantitative analysis of FL intensity at 5 µm depth. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001).

a) Schematic of the experimental protocols of S. aureus biofilm wound infection, treatment, and therapeutic effect evaluation. b) Representative wound photos of S. aureus biofilm-infected mice from various treatment groups, and corresponding LB agar plates photos of S. aureus colonies extracted from infected wound sites in different treatment groups on day 11 post-treatment. c) Simulated wound trace of infected wound areas over time after different treatments, and d) corresponding wound area statistics. e) Antibacterial efficiency of various treatments that are determined by the colonies counting results. f) H&E and Masson staining images of wound tissues collected from different treatment groups on day 11 post-treatment. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001).

head.ce:title: Phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dot-proteinase K nanohybrids for enhanced photodynamic therapy against bacterial biofilm infections

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Research Center for Photo-responsive Molecules and Materials School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Center for Photo-responsive Molecules and Materials School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInternational Joint Research Center for Photo-responsive Molecules and Materials School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Institute of Parasitic Diseases, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bJiangsu Institute of Parasitic Diseases, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanohybrids combining phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dots (PCDs) and proteinase K have been engineered for addressing the formidable challenges of antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) against bacterial biofilm infections, overcoming biofilm barrier obstruction, the limited diffusion of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and the inadequate ROS generation of traditional photosensitizers. PCDs are formulated for superior water solubility and robust singlet oxygen (1O2) production, mitigating issues related to dispersion and aggregation-induced quenching typical of conventional photosensitizers. The conjugation of phenylboronic acid to CDs not only enhanced 1O2 generation through increased electron-hole separation but also imparted strong bacterial binding capabilities to the PCDs, enabling broad-spectrum sterilization by maximizing the ROS-mediated bacterial destruction. Proteinase K, serving as a structural “glue”, actively breaks down biofilms and facilitates the deep penetration of functional PCDs, aiding effective treatment of biofilm infections. In vivo studies confirm that PCDs-proteinase K nanohybrids dramatically accelerate healing in biofilm-infected wounds by synergizing enhanced photosensitization, potent bacterial adherence, and efficient biofilm elimination and penetration. This approach highlights a straightforward strategy to significantly advance aPDT, promoting the clinical adoption of non-antibiotic methods for combating bacterial biofilm infections.

1) Phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dots (PCDs) were designed for enhanced water solubility and efficient singlet oxygen generation through surface modulation, also suggesting that surface modification can improve the inherent photosensitizing activity of CDs by promoting electron-hole separation; 2) The conjugation of phenylboronic acid endowed PCDs with strong bacterial binding capabilities, enabling highly efficient and broad-spectrum sterilization by maximizing reactive oxygen species-mediated bacterial destruction; 3) Incorporation of proteinase K (PK) leveraged its specific extracellular polymeric substance degrading capability, along with the stimuli-responsive release of PCDs from the PCDs-PK nanohybrids, facilitating biofilm breakdown and enabling deeper penetration of PCDs, thereby improving the treatment of biofilm infections

ce:section.ce:para: Battling bacterial infectious diseases has been a persistent challenge throughout human history, leading to high mortality rates, significant social problems, and economic losses [1–3]. Localized bacterial invasion with the opportunistic spread of bacterial biofilm infection is the primary cause of clinical infectious deaths [4,5]. Statistics show that over 80 % of bacterial infection wounds involve biofilm formation [6,7]. Biofilms are clusters of microbial cells encased in dense extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) [8], which are highly heterogeneous and complex, making it difficult for conventional antimicrobial drugs, such as antibiotics, to penetrate the deeper layers of the biofilm [9,10]. Additionally, the overuse of antibiotics has led to increased bacterial resistance [11], and the transfer of antibiotic-resistance genes via quorum sensing further promotes drug resistance [12–14]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for alternative antibacterial strategies to combat biofilm infections without inducing bacterial resistance.

Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) is a promising approach for eliminating infectious pathogens while minimizing the risk of drug resistance [15,16]. In aPDT, a photosensitizer is activated by light of a specific wavelength, undergoing an energy level jump to enter the excited state [17]. In this state, the photosensitizer interacts with oxygen or other substrates through energy transfer (Type II) or electron transfer (Type I) to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) [18,19]. These ROS cause nonspecific oxidative damage to critical cellular components such as lipids, proteins, and DNA, resulting in bacterial death with minimal resistance development [20,21]. Compared to mechanical and drug-based therapies, aPDT offers benefits such as non-invasiveness, temporal and spatial control, biocompatibility, and reduced drug resistance [22–24]. While aPDT has not yet been widely adopted in clinical practice, photodynamic therapy with a similar mechanism has been successfully used for treating skin cancers and providing palliative care for internal cancers with FDA-approved photosensitizers [25–27], underscoring the great potential of aPDT for treating bacterial infections in clinical settings.

Research on aPDT for bacterial biofilm infections is still in the exploratory phase. Various materials, including novel photosensitizers [28], metal-organic frameworks [29], hybrid microneedles [7], and nanocomposites [30], have been used to combat bacterial biofilm infections through ROS-induced oxidative damage. These studies highlight the advantages of aPDT in preventing the development of drug resistance and adapting well to bacterial infectious microenvironments. However, to advance the clinical application of aPDT against bacterial biofilm infections, several critical challenges must be addressed: i) The exclusive reliance on ROS to combat stubborn biofilms is often inadequate, leading to poor penetration of antibacterial agents into deeper biofilm layers. ii) The inherent limitations of ROS, including their short half-life and limited diffusion distance [31], coupled with the weak bacterial bonding capacity of most materials, resulting in suboptimal antibacterial effects. iii) The efficacy of ROS generation by conventional photosensitizers is further hindered by issues such as high hydrophobicity and aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). Consequently, there is a pressing need for novel aPDT platforms that integrate biofilm elimination and penetration, targeted bacterial binding, and improved photosensitization performance.

Herein, we developed a hybrid nanoplatform combining phenylboronic acid-modified carbon dots (PCDs) with proteinase K (PK) (referred as PCDs-PK nanohybrids), designed to tackle bacterial biofilm infections through enhanced photosensitization, strong bacterial adhesion, and effective biofilm disruption. This platform utilizes CDs known for their adaptive physicochemical traits, such as the photo-induced heat and ROS generation abilities [32,33], making them prime candidates for photothermal/photodynamic therapy applications [34,35]. Leveraging extensive expertise in CDs [36–39], we synthesized PCDs characterized by deep-red fluorescence (FL) emission, superior water solubility, and significant singlet oxygen (1O2) generation, establishing their role as potent nanophotosensitizers. The inclusion of the 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (PBA) group enhances microbial targeting, enabling PCDs to form borate esters with key bacterial wall components, lipopolysaccharides (LPS) in Gram-negative (G-) and teichoic acids in Gram-positive (G+) bacteria [40–42]. Additionally, PK is integrated for its formidable antibiofilm properties, it works by directly targeting and digesting specific proteinaceous molecules in the biofilm's EPS matrix [43], thus driving effective infection treatment strategies.

This goal-oriented design strategy yields several key advantages for the PCDs-PK nanohybrids: i) PCDs demonstrate exceptional water solubility and robust 1O2 production, addressing dispersion issues and ACQ common in traditional photosensitizers. This also indicates that surface modification could be a viable strategy to enhance the inherent photosensitization activity of CDs by improving electron-hole separation. ii) PCDs exhibit strong affinity to both G- and G+ bacteria via covalent bonding interactions, minimizing the action distance of ROS, enabling highly efficient and broad-spectrum sterilization by maximizing ROS-mediated bacterial destruction. iii) Incorporating PK leverages its specific EPS digesting capabilities and the stimuli-responsive release of PCDs from the PCDs-PK nanohybrids, ensuring active biofilm elimination and deeper penetration of functional PCDs for effective biofilm infection treatments. These desirable features position PCDs-PK nanohybrids as an easy-to-prepare platform for treating biofilm infections through comprehensively enhanced aPDT.

All solvents and reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Polyethyleneimine (PEI, molecular weight: 600), glutathione (GSH), acetonitrile, tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate, PBA, PK, crystal violet, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA) were acquired from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4-(4,6-dimethoxy-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)−4-methylmorpholinium chloride (DMTMM) and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). The Syto 9/PI live/dead bacterial double stain kit was obtained from Maokang Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was sourced from Saiguo Biotech Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Formamide was procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and DCFH-DA was purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

All bacterial strains used in this study were obtained from the Shanghai Bioresource Collection Center (Shanghai, China). S. aureus (ATCC 6538) was employed as a representative gram-positive bacterium, while E. coli (ATCC 25,922) and P. aeruginosa (ATCC 9027) served as representative gram-negative bacteria.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were captured using a JEM-2100PLUS microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired with a S-4800 microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a SHIMADZU UV-2600 spectrophotometer, while FL emission and excitation spectra were measured with a Hitachi FS5 spectrophotometer. Zeta potentials were measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern). The photodynamic performance of the CDs or PCDs was evaluated under irradiation from a 660 nm laser (Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co. Ltd., China). Photoelectrochemical measurements were conducted using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation. Bacterial images were captured with a Leica confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM TCS-SP8).

The CDs were prepared according to our previous report [44]. Briefly, 16.98 g of GSH and 10.56 g of PEI were dissolved in 300 mL of formamide. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 4 h After filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove larger particles, the solution was purified via dialysis against water for 7 days using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500 Da Finally, the CDs was collected by lyophilization as dark green powder.

PCDs were synthesized through an amide condensation reaction between CDs and PBA. Initially, 20 mg of PBA, 120.2 mg of DMTMM and 117.8 mg of DMAP were dissolved in water. Then, 100 mg of CDs were added to the solution and allowed to react in an oil bath at 40 °C for 24 h The solution was subsequently purified by dialysis (MWCO 1000 Da) against water for 3 days, and the final PCDs were collected by lyophilization.

PCDs-PK nanohybrids were synthesized through the intermolecular interactions between proteinase K and PCDs. Initially, 50 mg of PCDs and 25 mg of proteinase K were dissolved in water and stirred at room temperature for 2 h The solution was then purified by ultrafiltration, and the final PCDs-PK nanohybrids were collected by lyophilization.

The generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) in an aqueous solution was assessed using ABDA as an indicator. In this experiment, 2 mL of PCDs (25 μg mL−1) and CDs (25 μg mL−1) were placed in a cuvette, with ABDA (0.15 mM) added subsequently. The sample was then exposed to a 660 nm laser at a power density of 100 mW cm−2 for 20 min. The UV absorption of ABDA was measured at various intervals during the irradiation to monitor changes over time.

In brief, Chlorin e6 (Ce6, Φ = 0.6) was employed as a standard for the evaluation of 1O2 production by CDs and PCDs. SOSG was utilized as the 1O2 indicator. SOSG (2.5 µM) was added into Ce6, CDs, and PCDs solutions (all at the same concentration of 10 µg mL−1), followed by laser irradiation (660 nm, 30 mW cm−2) for different times (0–2 min). The quantum yield of 1O2 production by various samples was calculated by the following formula
Φ
x
=
Φ
C e 6
K x
A
C e 6
/
(
K
C e 6
A x
)
in which K Ce6 and K x represent the slopes of calibration curves obtained from Figures S11-S13, Supporting Information, respectively. A Ce6 and A x are the absorption values of Ce6 and CDs or PCDs at 660 nm, respectively.

For the preparation of photoelectrodes, solutions containing CDs and PCDs were deposited onto indium tin oxide (ITO) substrates (1 cm2) and allowed to dry at room temperature. The photoelectrochemical measurements were conducted using a conventional three-electrode setup, with the ITO electrode as the working electrode, a platinum counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The electrolyte solution used was 50 mM tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate.

Photocurrent measurements, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were performed using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation. Illumination was provided by a 300 W incandescent lamp equipped with a UV cut-off filter (λ > 400 nm). Tauc plots were generated using the equation:
(
α h v
)
1 / n
= A
(
h v − E g
)
Where α represents the absorption coefficient, h denotes Planck's constant, ν signifies the optical frequency, Eg represents the band gap energy, and A denotes the coefficient.

The antimicrobial activity of PBA, CDs, and PCDs was evaluated using a plate counting method. Briefly, 300 µL of a bacterial suspension (105 CFU mL−1) was added to centrifuge tubes, followed by the addition of PBA (80 µg mL−1), CDs (400 µg mL−1), and PCDs (400 µg mL−1) to the respective samples. The mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. For the samples that underwent laser treatment, they were exposed to 660 nm laser light at a power of 300 mW cm−1 for 10 min. Subsequently, 100 µL of each solution was spread on agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h The antimicrobial efficacy was calculated using the following formula:
Anti bact erial
effi cien cy
( % )
= 1 −
CF
U
samp le
CF
U
cont rol
×
100

The antimicrobial efficacy of CDs and PCDs was further evaluated using live/dead FL staining with Syto 9 (green FL) and propidium iodide (PI, red FL). Bacterial suspensions (1 × 107 CFU mL−1) were initially incubated with saline, CDs (400 µg mL−1), or PCDs (400 µg mL−1) for 10 min. For the laser-treated group, the samples were then exposed to 660 nm laser irradiation at a power density of 300 mW cm−2 for 10 min. After irradiation, the bacteria were stained with Syto 9 (50 µM) and PI (1 mg mL−1) for 10 min at 37 °C in darkness, followed by imaging using inverted FL microscope.

Initially, the bacteria were incubated with saline, CDs (400 µg mL−1), or PCDs (400 µg mL−1) for 10 min. After incubation, the samples were fixed with paraformaldehyde for 48 h The bacteria were then dehydrated using a graded ethanol series (50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, 95 %, and 100 %). Finally, the bacterial morphology was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

ROS levels within bacteria were measured using the ROS probe DCFH-DA. Bacterial suspensions were first co-incubated with saline, CDs (100 µg mL−1), or PCDs (100 µg mL−1) for 3 h Subsequently, DCFH-DA (10 μM) was added to the samples and incubated for an additional 30 min. The laser-treated group was then exposed to a 660 nm laser at 300 mW cm−2 for 10 min. Finally, the samples were placed on slides and analyzed using CLSM.

S. aureus was used as the model bacterium for biofilm formation. Initially, 200 μL of a bacterial suspension (1 × 108 CFU mL−1), diluted in LB medium, was added to a sterile, flat-bottomed 96-well polystyrene microtiter plate. The plate was incubated at 37 °C for 48 h to allow biofilm formation. After incubation, the bacterial suspension was removed, and the wells were washed three times with saline. PCDs-PK nanohybrids and proteinase K were each introduced into the biofilm and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h For the laser-treated group, samples were then exposed to a 660 nm laser at 300 mW cm−2 for 10 min. Next, 200 μL of 0.1 % crystal violet solution was added to the wells and incubated at room temperature for 20 min. The wells were then rinsed with running water to remove excess crystal violet. Finally, 200 μL of 33 % acetic acid was added and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. The absorbance at 590 nm was measured using a microplate reader to quantify the biofilm biomass.

Following the method described above, mature S. aureus biofilms were cultured on slides. PCDs, PCDs-PK nanohybrids, and proteinase K were then individually introduced into the S. aureus biofilms and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h After incubation, the samples were exposed to a 660 nm laser at 300 mW cm−2 for 10 min. Post-laser treatment, the samples were stained with Syto 9 (50 μM) and PI (1 mg mL−1) for 10 min at 37 °C under dark conditions. Finally, the samples were observed using CLSM, and their fluorescent 3D images were captured.

The human immortalized keratinocytes (HaCaT) cells (National Collection of Authenticated Cell Cultures, Shanghai, China) were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 2 × 10⁴ cells per well and incubated overnight to allow for cell adherence. The cells were then treated with varying concentrations of PCDs (100–500 µg mL⁻¹) for 24 h. After incubation, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing 10 μL of CCK-8 solution, and the cells were incubated for an additional 4 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was then measured using a microplate reader.

Male Balb/c mice (6–8 weeks old) obtained from Cavins Laboratory Animals Ltd. (Changzhou, China) were used for the in vivo experiments. All animal care and treatment procedures followed the “Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals in China” and received approval from the Animal Experiment Regional Ethics Committee of Jiangsu Institute of Parasitic Diseases, China (License Number: JIPD-IACUC-2,023,139). To evaluate the in vivo efficacy of PCDs-PK nanohybrids for treating biofilm infections, a wound infection model was established. A 1 cm diameter wound was created on the back of each mouse using a scalpel, followed by infection with a 200 µL suspension of S. aureus. After two days of infection, a uniform white film formed on the wound surface, indicating the successful construction of a biofilm-infected wound. The mice were randomly divided into three groups (n = 4 per group, one was sacrificed for bacterial counting during observation period) and treated with either saline, PCDs, or PCDs-PK nanohybrid dispersions, which were uniformly applied to the wound area, followed by irradiation with a 660 nm laser at 300 mW cm−2 for 10 min. Wound healing was monitored by capturing images every two days until day 11.

At the end of the observation period, representative mice from each treatment group were sacrificed, and skin tissues from the infected areas were collected for histological analysis. The tissue samples were first washed with saline, then fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde, followed by embedding, sectioning, and staining with H&E and Masson for further examination.

All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analyses were performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with GraphPad software (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001, n.s. indicates not significant).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PCDs-PK nanohybrids
Enhanced photosensitization performance of PCDs and underlying mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The modification of PBA on CDs was intended not only for targeted sterilization via binding reactions but also for enhanced photosensitization through surface modulation. Thus, the deep-red light-triggered 1O2 production from CDs and PCDs was thoroughly studied using 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA) as a sensitive indicator (Fig. 2a ). The typical absorption of ABDA at 400 nm gradually decreased in the presence of both materials when exposed to 660 nm laser irradiation over time (Figure S9), indicating ROS generation. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 2b, the absorption reduction induced by PCDs was significantly greater than that of free CDs, demonstrating the improved 1O2 generation ability of PCDs. To further assess the impact of hybridization on photosensitization performance of PCDs, the light-triggered 1O2 production by PCDs-PK nanohybrids was evaluated. As depicted in Figure S10, the characteristic ABDA absorption at 400 nm gradually decreased at a rate comparable to that of PCDs alone, indicating negligible influence on photosensitization performance after hybridization. To quantify the enhancement in photosensitization activity, the quantum efficiency of 1O2 was assessed using Chlorin e6 (Ce6) as a standard reference. The quantum yield of PCDs was determined to be 0.42, which was approximately twice the value of free CDs, around 0.21 (Figures S11-S13). Transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted to elucidate the mechanism behind the enhanced photosensitization performance of PBA-conjugated CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, PCDs exhibited significantly higher photocurrent responses compared to CDs during ten on–off cycles, indicating improved photo-induced charge separation efficiency in PCDs. The EIS results presented in Fig. 2d revealed a larger Nyquist plot radius for PCDs in contrast with free CDs, suggesting a reduced charge transfer resistance for PCDs. These findings demonstrate that the modification of PBA on CDs facilitates photo-activated electron-hole separation, thereby enhancing the photosensitization capability of PCDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectra and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were subsequently performed to investigate the energy-band characteristics of CDs and PCDs. The bandgap energy (Eg) of these materials was determined from the UV–Vis absorption spectra. As shown in Figs. 2e and 2f, the Eg values of CDs and PCDs were calculated to be 1.70 and 1.66 eV, respectively, based on the Tauc method. Electrodes with deposited CDs and PCDs were prepared for CV measurements. As depicted in Figs. 2 g and 2h, the conduction band energy level (ECB) of CDs and PCDs were found to be −1.16 and −0.82 eV (vs Ag/AgCl), respectively. The valence band energy levels (EVB) were calculated to be 0.54 and 0.84 eV (vs Ag/AgCl), respectively, using the equation EVB = ECB + Eg. A simplified energy band diagram of CDs and PCDs was depicted based on these findings. As illustrated in Fig. 2i, PCDs demonstrated more effective electron-hole separation and possessed a narrower Eg with a lower ECB position compared to CDs, which contributes to the enhancement of 1O2 production. Additionally, transient FL spectroscopy measurements were performed for both CDs and PCDs. As shown in Figure S14, the average FL lifetimes were determined to be 3.28 ns for CDs and 5.27 ns for PCDs, based on the fitted FL decay curves. The extended lifetime observed for PCDs indicates prolonged triplet lifetimes, contributing to their enhanced photosensitization activity. Thus, surface modification has shown its potential in strengthening the intrinsic photosensitization activity of CDs as a generalized strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic antibacterial activity of PCDs and the antibacterial mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: After confirming the enhanced photosensitization performance of PCDs, their targeted sterilization effect facilitated by the coupled PBA were thoroughly investigated. Typical pathogenic bacteria, including Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Escherichia coli (E. coli), and drug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), were selected for the antibacterial assay using the classical plate count method. CDs without surface modification served as a reference for comparing the antibacterial performance of PCDs. As shown in Figs. 3a –3d, PBA, CDs, and PCDs without laser irradiation induced negligible bacterial killing. Upon exposure to 660 nm laser irradiation (300 mW cm−2, 10 min), CDs exhibited considerable sterilization effects due to their photosensitization properties. However, the antibacterial efficiency varied significantly between G+ and G- strains. While aPDT is considered a noninvasive, broad-spectrum antibacterial strategy with low resistance risk, its practical application often exhibits selectivity for G+ bacteria. This selectivity is mainly attributed to the different membrane structures of G+ and G- bacteria [45]. Notably, PCDs demonstrated a highly efficient and non-discriminatory sterilization effect, significantly reducing the survival rates of all types of bacteria compared to CDs. The antibacterial activity of PCDs-PK nanohybrids was further evaluated using E. coli as a representative strain. As shown in Figure S15, the group treated with PCDs-PK nanohybrids combined with laser irradiation exhibited efficient antibacterial activity, comparable to that of free PCDs, thereby confirming the preserved aPDT capability. Additionally, The truly broad-spectrum antibacterial activity of PCDs was further confirmed through live/dead assays and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. Syto 9 (green FL for all bacteria) and propidium iodide (PI) (red FL for dead bacteria) co-staining experiments were conducted using S. aureus and E. coli as representatives, analyzed via confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 3e, noticeable red FL was observed in bacteria treated with CDs and exposed to laser irradiation. In contrast, almost all bacteria in the PCDs and laser treatment groups were dead, displaying predominant red FL. These findings align with the plate count experiment results. SEM images in Fig. 3f revealed apparent morphological deformation and membrane rupture in bacteria treated with CDs and PCDs after laser exposure, compared to the smooth surfaces and intact membranes of bacteria in the control group. As expected, more severe wrinkling and membrane distortion were observed in the PCDs treatment groups. Overall, PCDs exhibit greatly improved photodynamic inactivation abilities and hold potential as a truly broad-spectrum antibacterial agent, effective even against drug-resistant strains. The enhanced antibacterial effect of PCDs can be attributed to the covalent interaction of the PBA group with cis-dihydroxy groups on the bacterial surface (such as the teichoic acid of G+ bacteria and LPS of G- bacteria, Fig. 3g) and the increased 1O2 generation capability of PCDs.

To verify the proposed antibacterial mechanism, the interaction between bacteria and the prepared CDs, as well as their 1O2 generation capacity at the bacterial level, were evaluated. Utilizing the deep-red emissive property of CDs and PCDs, CLSM and flow cytometry measurements were conducted to clarify their bacterial adhesion and internalization performances. As shown in Fig. 4a , brighter FL signals were observed in the PCDs-treated group compared to the CDs-treated group. The corresponding quantitative results (Fig. 4b) showed a significant difference in average FL intensity between CDs and PCDs-treated bacteria, indicating increased bacterial adhesion and internalization by PCDs. Flow cytometry analysis (Fig. 4c) further confirmed that PCDs led to a noticeable increase in deep red FL in both G+ and G- bacteria. The ROS levels within bacteria after different treatments were analyzed using 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as the FL indicator. As illustrated in Figs. 4d and 4e, only faint FL was observed in bacteria treated with CDs and PCDs alone, while observable FL appeared in the CDs and laser-treated groups, indicating photo-induced 1O2 generation by CDs. As expected, the strongest green FL was observed in bacteria treated with PCDs followed by laser exposure, due to increased bacterial adhesion and enhanced photosensitization efficiency of PCDs. Quantitative analysis showed that the average FL intensity of PCDs and laser-treated S. aureus and E. coli was 2.5 and 4.2 times higher, respectively, than that of CDs and laser-treated groups. These findings demonstrate that the surface modification with PBA facilitates targeted and efficient photodynamic sterilization, resulting from the strong bacterial binding via the borate ester covalent bond and strengthened intrinsic photosensitization activity of PCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofilm elimination and penetration abilities of PCDs-PK nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of bacterial biofilms creates a stubborn barrier to the application of aPDT. In this study, PK was employed to degrade the bacterial extracellular matrix, thereby promoting the penetration of functional PCDs. To verify this goal-oriented design, S. aureus biofilms were first established and treated with various concentrations of PCDs-PK nanohybrids. The crystal violet staining assay was used to evaluate biofilm elimination efficacy by measuring the biomass of the biofilm. As shown in Figs. 5a and 5b, significant color changes from violet to pale violet were observed as the PCDs-PK nanohybrids concentration increased, demonstrating their superior biofilm elimination ability. The corresponding biofilm removal rate reached up to 73.9 % at a PCDs-PK nanohybrids concentration of 500 µg mL−1, comparable to the antibiofilm effect of PK at an equivalent concentration. The effective penetration of functional PCDs leads to the killing of deep-seated bacteria protected by biofilm. To verify the antibiofilm performance and biofilm penetration ability of PCDs-PK nanohybrids, live/dead staining was employed. S. aureus biofilms stained with Syto 9 and PI were treated with PCDs and PCDs-PK nanohybrids, with and without laser irradiation, respectively. Fluorescent photographs were captured using the 3D function of CLSM. As shown in Fig. 5c, biofilms treated with PCDs and laser exhibited noticeable red FL, indicating some degree of biofilm damage due to 1O2 production and PCDs penetration in the surface layer. As expected, bright red FL was observed in biofilms treated with PCDs-PK nanohybrids and laser, especially in the longitudinal section. This suggests a strong antibiofilm effect and effective penetration of PCDs facilitated by the PK component. Photos in the PI channel at different depths were selected to provide clearer evidence of PK-enhanced biofilm permeability of the photosensitive unit. As presented in Fig. 5d, intense red FL was observed in the biofilm treated with PCDs-PK nanohybrids and laser, even at a deeper depth of 5 µm, whereas only a faint signal was recorded in the biofilm exposed to PCDs and laser. Quantitative analyses (Fig. 5e and Figure S16) revealed that PCDs-PK nanohybrids-treated group exhibited significantly higher average FL intensities across all detected layers compared to the PCDs-treated group and the corresponding signal intensities even differed by approximately 11.8 times at the depth of 5 µm. These results demonstrated the effective biofilm elimination and penetration abilities of PCDs-PK nanohybrids as designed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biofilm infection treatment performance of PCDs-PK nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: In light of the effective biofilm elimination and penetration properties, targeted photodynamic antibacterial activity, and enhanced 1O2 generation efficacy of PCDs-PK nanohybrids, their performance in treating in vivo biofilm infections was studied. To ensure in vivo application safety, the cytotoxicity and blood compatibility of functional PCDs were evaluated first. As shown in Figure S17, negligible cytotoxicity was observed in HaCaT cells, even at the highest concentration of 500 μg mL−1, indicating superior cytocompatibility. Figure S18 also showed no significant red blood cell damage in PCDs-treated samples compared to the positive control group treated with water. Additionally, UV–vis absorption results revealed that the hemolysis rates of PCDs-incubated samples were similar to that of PBS-treated sample, indicating favorable hemocompatibility. To assess the biofilm infection treatment capacity of PCDs-PK nanohybrids, full-thickness skin wounds with mixed biofilm infections were modeled on the backs of mice and subjected to different treatments. Experimental protocols, including wound infection, treatment, and evaluation of therapeutic effects, are presented in Fig. 6a . Mice with visible biofilm at wound sites were randomly assigned to three treatment groups: i) laser irradiation (660 nm, 300 mW cm−2, 10 min); ii) PCDs + laser; iii) PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser. Wound photos of representative mice from each treatment group were taken regularly, and the corresponding wound traces were recorded throughout the monitoring period (Figs. 6b and 6c). Wounds in the PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser treatment group showed faster healing and size reduction compared to those in the other groups. The wound closure rate in the PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser treatment group reached 94.77 % by day 11, compared to 69.18 % in the control group and 85.16 % in the PCDs + laser-treated group (Figs. 6d). To evaluate the biofilm bacteria elimination capacity, residual bacteria within the wound sites were collected on day 7 post-treatment and cultured for colony counting. As illustrated in the far-right column of Fig. 6b and Fig. 6e, the fewest bacterial colonies were observed in the PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser treatment group, indicating effective biofilm infection clearance and low recurrence risk. Finally, wound tissues from various treatment groups were collected for histological analysis via H&E and Masson staining assays (Figs. 6f). Biofilm-infected wounds in the control group exhibited a natural healing process, marked by large amounts of bruising under the epidermis (black arrow), extensive inflammatory cell infiltration (yellow arrow), and the absence of hair follicles. Conversely, tissues in the PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser-treated group showed restored epithelial tissue and regenerated hair follicles (red arrow), indicating significant recovery of wound tissue function. According to the Masson staining results, deeper blue coloration was found in the PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser treatment group, suggesting higher collagen content. Given collagen's vital role in dermal extracellular matrix reorganization and tissue remodeling, the increased collagen content following PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser treatment likely facilitates efficient wound healing. Statistical analyses of wound granulation tissue thickness and collagen deposition further validated the aforementioned results. As illustrated in Figure S19 in the revised Supporting Information, the PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser-treated group demonstrated the highest granulation tissue thickness and collagen area fraction compared to other groups. Finally, the in vivo safety of PCDs-PK nanohybrids was evaluated via H&E staining assay. As shown in Figure S20, no significant inflammatory response or tissue damage was observed in the PCDs-PK nanohybrids + laser treatment group compared to the control group, indicating minimal toxic side effects of PCDs-PK nanohybrids. Therefore, PCDs-PK nanohybrids, with their combined capabilities of biofilm elimination and penetration, and enhanced photodynamic sterilization, offer a promising approach for biofilm-infected wounds healing.

In summary, we have successfully developed versatile nanohybrids of PCDs-PK with enhanced photosensitization, strong bacterial binding, and effective biofilm elimination and penetration capabilities, specifically tailored for combating bacterial biofilm infections. PCDs, characterized by their deep-red FL emission, significant 1O2 generation and outstanding water solubility, serving dual roles as the nanophotosensitizer and FL indicator. Notably, the modification of CDs with PBA not only enhanced their 1O2 generation through improved electron-hole separation efficiency but also endowed the PCDs with a robust bacterial binding capacity, ensuring highly efficient and broad-spectrum sterilization. In vitro experiments demonstrated that PCDs are effective against a variety of bacteria, including multidrug-resistant strains, and exhibited strong affinity to both G+ and G- bacteria, leading to increased bacterial ROS levels. The integration of PK facilitated active biofilm elimination and deeper penetration of functional PCDs, enhancing biofilm infection treatment. In vivo studies further showed that PCDs-PK nanohybrids dramatically improved the healing of biofilm-infected wounds. This approach offers a promising strategy to overcome key challenges in aPDT and supports the clinical adaptation of non-antibiotic treatments for bacterial biofilm infections.

Haoyi Sun: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Henggang Wang: Project administration, Data curation. Ke Cheng: Investigation. Yonghua Zhou: Investigation, Methodology. Xinxin Wang: Project administration. Shang Gao: Project administration. Jinhong Mo: Project administration. Si Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Hengwei Lin: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Peng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52173126 and 32101142), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2024M751152 and 2023M731345), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20221203) and Top Talent Support Program for young and middle-aged people of Wuxi Health Committee (BJ2023043).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.actbio.2025.01.030.
